prompt: Alexander Graham Bell is credited with inventing what revolutionary device in 1876?
Percy Spencer accidentally invented the microwave oven when he noticed his chocolate bar melting near what kind of device?
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 after returning from a break to find mould had killed bacteria in his petri dish. Where had he been?
options: Recovering from the flu at home
Working night shifts at another hospital

prompt: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at which research organisation?
Georges de Mestral invented Velcro in 1948 after noticing how something kept sticking to his dog's fur during a walk. What was it?
Roy Plunkett accidentally invented Teflon in 1938 at DuPont while searching for what?
options: A new type of engine lubricant

prompt: Walter Hunt invented the safety pin in 1849 to pay off a $15 debt — then sold the patent rights for how much?
Coca-Cola was first sold in 1886 by pharmacist John Pemberton. What specific event directly triggered him to reformulate his wine-based tonic into a non-alcoholic drink?
options: A business partner insisted on removing alcohol
He wanted to market it to children
Atlanta and Fulton County enacted a local alcohol prohibition
A federal law banned cocaine-laced wines

prompt: Spencer Silver accidentally invented the weak, repositionable adhesive behind Post-it notes in 1968 — but it sat unused for years. What was he actually trying to create?
options: A heat-resistant sealant for jet engines
A strong permanent adhesive for aerospace use

prompt: Stephanie Kwolek invented Kevlar at DuPont in 1965. The project that led her there was a search for a lightweight fibre to replace what material in car tyres?
Alfred Nobel's 1867 breakthrough was mixing volatile nitroglycerin with kieselguhr to make it safe to handle. What exactly is kieselguhr?
The claim that Dom Pérignon invented champagne is largely a myth. Who first formally documented adding sugar to wine to trigger a deliberate second fermentation — and in what year?
Thomas Edison is famous for perfecting which everyday device, which he patented in 1879?
The Wright brothers, who built the first successful powered aeroplane, ran what kind of shop before they took to the skies?
Which squishy childhood favourite was originally invented as a cleaning product for wallpaper before being rebranded as a toy?
Johannes Gutenberg's 15th-century invention transformed the world by mass-producing what?
The popsicle was reportedly invented by an 11-year-old who left a cup of soda powder, water, and a stirring stick outside overnight. What froze it solid?
Which of these breakfast foods was invented by the Kellogg brothers, partly as a bland health food meant to discourage certain urges?
The QWERTY keyboard layout we still use today was originally designed for which machine?
Ruth Wakefield, who ran a Massachusetts inn, created the chocolate chip cookie. Famously, she gave Nestlé the rights to print her recipe in exchange for what?
Garrett Morgan, an early-20th-century inventor, patented an improved version of which life-saving device, plus an early traffic signal?
In 1903, decades before it became standard, Mary Anderson patented a hand-operated version of which car accessory after watching a streetcar driver struggle in the snow?
Wilson Greatbatch accidentally invented the implantable cardiac pacemaker when he grabbed the wrong-sized component while building what?
The Frisbee got its name from the Frisbie Pie Company, whose empty tins college students liked to do what with?
Hollywood actress Hedy Lamarr co-invented a 'frequency-hopping' system during WWII that helped lay the groundwork for which modern technology?
Potato chips were supposedly invented by chef George Crum when a customer kept sending back his fried potatoes, complaining they were too what?
WD-40, the famous spray lubricant, got its odd name because it was developed to do what — on the 40th attempt?
Margaret Knight won a patent battle in the 1870s against a man who stole her design for a machine that made what still-used item?
Saccharin, the first artificial sweetener, was discovered in 1879 when chemist Constantin Fahlberg noticed an odd sweetness. Where did he taste it?
options: On his own bread at dinner, from unwashed lab residue
In a spilled beaker of tea

prompt: Charles Goodyear spent years and went into debt perfecting vulcanised rubber. His crucial 1839 breakthrough came when he accidentally did what to a rubber-and-sulphur mixture?
options: Dropped it onto a hot stove

prompt: Percy Shaw invented the 'cat's eye' road reflector in the 1930s. According to the popular account, what inspired the design?
options: The eyes of an actual cat caught in his headlights
Glass beads in a children's toy

prompt: The Super Soaker water gun was invented by Lonnie Johnson, an aerospace engineer, while he was tinkering at home on a project related to what?
Fed up with smudgy fountain pens, Hungarian journalist László Bíró perfected which everyday writing tool, now nicknamed after him in many countries?
Elisha Otis didn't invent the elevator, but his 1850s demonstration made people trust it. What did his invention do?
options: Stopped it from falling if the cable snapped

prompt: The humble zip fastener struggled for decades until the 1920s, when one product made it a hit. What was that breakthrough product?
What did Wilhelm Röntgen accidentally discover in 1895, earning the very first Nobel Prize in Physics?
Bubble Wrap was invented in 1957 when two engineers tried to make a trendy product that totally flopped. What were they originally trying to sell?
Samuel Morse co-developed a code of dots and dashes for which communication technology?
When Clarence Birdseye revolutionised food in the 1920s, what was his big idea?
Sylvan Goldman invented the shopping cart in 1937, but shoppers refused to use it at first. How did he get them to try it?
options: He gave a cash discount to anyone who used one
He banned hand baskets from his stores
He made the first carts out of solid gold-coloured metal
He hired models to push carts around as if shopping

prompt: The first practical photographic process, the daguerreotype, captured an image onto a polished plate of which material?
Josephine Cochrane invented a workable dishwasher in 1886 not to save labour, but for a very specific personal reason. What was it?
options: Her well water was too cold for handwashing
She was tired of her servants chipping her fine china
She wanted to win a county fair prize
She had injured her hands and couldn't wash up

prompt: The parking meter, first installed in Oklahoma City in 1935, was invented partly to solve what problem for local businesses?
options: Horses and cars sharing the same lanes
Workers hogging spaces all day so customers couldn't park
People parking on the wrong side of the road

prompt: Chester Carlson invented the photocopier, melting sulphur on his kitchen stove to make the first copy in 1938. The process was named 'xerography' — from the Greek for what?
Naval engineer Richard James got the idea for the Slinky in 1943 after knocking a tension spring off a shelf in his shipyard workshop. What did the spring do that amazed him?
options: It coiled itself around a pencil
It 'stepped' end over end down to the floor and stood upright
It rolled across the floor like a hoop
It bounced back up onto the shelf

prompt: Barbed wire, patented by Joseph Glidden in 1874, had such a huge effect on the American West that it earned what nickname?
Earl Tupper's airtight Tupperware containers sold poorly in shops until Brownie Wise pioneered which sales method to save them?
options: Selling them only in hardware stores
Giving them away free with groceries

prompt: The pop-up toaster, patented by Charles Strite in the early 1920s, was reportedly born out of his frustration at work. What kept happening?
options: His army unit had no way to toast rations
His wife's toaster shocked her every morning
He couldn't afford bread that wasn't stale
The cafeteria at his factory kept serving burnt toast

prompt: Earle Dickson invented the Band-Aid in 1920 while working as a cotton buyer at Johnson & Johnson. What prompted him?
options: His own job left his hands constantly nicked by cotton bales
His factory kept losing workers to minor cuts
He was a volunteer medic with no supplies
His wife Josephine kept cutting herself while cooking

prompt: A cavity magnetron, the device that gave Allied radar its punch, was secretly carried to the United States in 1940 inside a black metal box during which mission?
Patsy Sherman co-invented Scotchgard fabric protector at 3M after a lab assistant spilled a chemical and noticed what?
options: It made a canvas shoe permanently stiff
It glowed faintly under UV light
It dissolved the rubber sole of the shoe
The spot it landed on repelled water and wouldn't stain

prompt: Zenith's Flash-Matic of 1955 was the first wireless TV remote, firing a beam of light at sensors in the screen's corners. What embarrassing flaw did it have?
options: Bright sunlight could change the channel all by itself
It had to be pointed at the ceiling to work
It only worked in total darkness
It drained a fresh battery in a single evening

prompt: The rugged 'jerry can' fuel container copied by the Allies in WWII got its nickname from what?
options: The Germans, nicknamed 'Jerries', who designed it
Its inventor, a man named Jerry
A jelly-like seal inside the lid

prompt: IBM's 'Simon', released in 1994, combined a phone, a touchscreen, and apps like a calculator and calendar. It's now widely called the first what?
The QR code, now scanned billions of times a day for menus and payments, was invented in 1994 by a company in which industry?
options: The automotive industry, to track car parts

prompt: The LED, now in nearly every screen and light bulb, is far more efficient than the old filament bulb because it produces light by doing what?
options: Burning a tiny amount of gas
Heating a metal filament until it glows
Passing current through a semiconductor that emits light directly
Spinning a magnet to create sparks

prompt: Igor Sikorsky, the Kyiv-born aviation pioneer, is most famous for building the first mass-produced, practical version of which aircraft in the United States?
The lithium-ion battery that powers nearly every phone and laptop earned its three key developers which prize in 2019?
The barcode was dreamed up by Norman Joseph Woodland, who first sketched the idea in the sand of a beach as a visual extension of what?
The 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry went to Emmanuelle Charpentier and Jennifer Doudna for developing CRISPR — the 'genetic scissors' that edit DNA. What made that award historic?
options: It was the first Nobel ever shared by two women
It was awarded just one year after the discovery
Both winners refused the prize money
It was the first Nobel given for biology

prompt: The shipping container reshaped global trade after trucking magnate Malcolm McLean standardized it in the 1950s. What kind of vessel was his very first container ship, the Ideal X?
The Haber-Bosch process, credited with feeding roughly half of humanity by making synthetic fertilizer, pulls nitrogen from the air to manufacture what key product?
